title: The Macro Minute: How are Q2 earnings shaping up? What is the risk of Japan’s LDP losing parliament?

Howdy team 42. It's your skipper here at
Darius Dell to present our macro minute
for Monday July 21st 2025. Hope everyone
had a wonderful weekend. So as always
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadoff morning note. If
you would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following KISS and Dr. Mo, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So today's
key macro questions are how is Q2
earnings season shaping up thus far and
what is the key risk of Japan's LDP
losing its seven decade strangle hold on
Japanese parliament. Our answers Q2
earnings season kicked off strong last
week as the banks crushed expectations
and multiple airlines signaled resilient
travel demand forcing bears into the
upside capitulation we've been calling
for since May 3rd. This is all
consistent with our three-year-old
resilient US economy theme and our
three-month-old paradigm C theme. Both
themes call for both macro and micro
data to continue generally surprising to
the upside over the medium to long term,
which is 3 to 12 plus months in our risk
management normal picture. With 60
companies having reported thus far, S&P
500 constituents are reporting modestly
decelerating sales growth of 4.4% 4%
down from 5% to Q1 and the highest
percentage of positive surprises since 1
Q2023 at 66.1%.
S&P 500 constituents are reporting a
material deceleration in earnings growth
to 3.3% down from 13.6% in Q1 and also
the highest percentage of positive
surprises since 2Q21 at 83.1%.
Despite losing control of both
parliamentary chambers for the first
time in the Liberal Democratic Party's
70-year history, Japanese Prime Minister
Shagirro Oshiba vowed to stay in power
Monday. In a twist of political irony,
Ashiba, who called for then Prime
Minister Shinszo AB's resignation in
2007, was reminded Monday of his past
demand that Abbe quote unquote explain
himself to the public as well as to the
party. 18 years later, the LDP carousel
spins once again with the Sheibba in the
hot seat and needing to score political
wins to maintain power. Ashiba seeks
quote unquote tangible results from US
trade talks and dispatch longtime deputy
Rosia Akazawa to Washington for an
eighth round of negotiations.
The Sheibba's desperation may cause him
to misjudge the Trump administration's
willingness to soften terms in trade
negotiations which may ultimately result
in higher tariffs and increase trade
policy uncertainty over a short-term
time horizon which is uh less than one
month in our risk management plan.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
is always wrap up the question from our
community. Uh this one's um just starts

where I can ask your question but I was
listening to Tuesday's macro minute and
you've reiterated numerous times
rightfully so about paradigm C however
you overlay the idea that the federal
reserve should be taken into account
information and cutting the Fed funds uh
right fed funds rate and I just don't
understand that at all exactly why with
unemployment around 4% and economic
growth running at around 2% plus
financial conditions according to my
weekly update from the Chicago Federal
Reserve Board have done nothing but
loosen for months and months on in the
rapid speculation, the stock market at
an all-time high. Exactly why should the
Federal Reserve be cutting short-term
interest rates? 40 years of investing
tells me anything maybe they should be
increasing short-term interest rates.
So, um you know, we just have a
different take on that uh in our view.
Uh so, uh just with with regards to the
unemployment rate, uh recall that we had
a significant change in in in
immigration policy uh at the start of
the year with the incoming Trump
administration. We essentially locked
down the border. If you look at legal
border crossings, which are tracking
kind of in the 250,000 per month range,
we're now tracking kind of in the the 20
less than 20,000 per month range. And I
want to say the most recent month of
data, which I believe is June, uh, we
saw essentially almost no illegal border
crossings. And so, and now we're
obviously starting to see some
deportations as well. So, all that
reduction in the growth rate of labor
supply, if not outright negative,
outright contraction labor supply is
keeping a a it's depressing the
unemployment rate. So the unemployment
rate right now and will be increasingly
so as we move forward in time with this
same uh uh uh tighter immigration
enforcement policy is going to be
artificially depressed relative to what
it otherwise would be in the context of
the degra the degradation and softening
we continue to observe in the labor
market. Uh I would say the most
important statistic from the uh June
jobs report that we got a couple few
weeks ago uh was the fact that private
sector labor income growth slowed to
1.4% on a three-month annualized rate of
change basis. uh that 1.4% is uh 390
basis points lower than the pre-COVID
trend of of private sector labor income
growth. Uh that 370 basis point
deceleration from 5.1% through meth
annualized to 1.4% through methan
annualized June was the sharpest
deceleration since May of 202022. And
that 1.4% through methan annualized
growth rate in private sector labor
income was the lowest level we've seen
since June of 2020. since the lockdowns
of June of 2020. And so we had a
significant and sharp deceleration in
private sector labor income growth
against sharpest deceleration we've seen
since May of 2022 to the lowest level
we've seen since June of 2020 tells you
that there was a you know we're already
starting to see a a very clear evidence
of our U-shaped economy theme in the
labor market. So, in our view, it it
does behoove the Fed to actually uh get
on with it. Uh because I think they're
making a what I think is a very flawed
and I think what a lot of strategists,
economists, investors view as a flawed
argument that hey, we got to wait and
see on tariffs to see if they're going
to be inflationary or not. We know
tariffs aren't inflationary. There's a
legions of academic literature that says
tariffs are one-time price level change.
They're one-off shock to the to the
general price level. This is not
inflation. the Fed's policy rate has
nothing to do it's not going to be able
to impact tariff inflation at all um
this oneoff price level change to the
upside or downside. So the Fed should
ignore the impact of tariffs and just
look at what the underlying inflation
trends inflation data are suggesting and
more importantly what the underlying uh
uh macroeconomic data is suggesting as
it relates to the outlook for the labor
market. I mean, this is this the June
data tells you that the labor market is
as bad as it's been in delta terms since
May of 2022 when we had a technical
recession and since June of 2020 when we
had a actual recession at least with the
lockdowns in COVID. So that's in our
opinion I think the Fed would do well uh
to to to start to lower the policy rate
again. But again, we've opened up of the
view for years that we are the most
important thing that the Fed needs to do
in this fourth turning regime is to
support fiscal balance. And so I think
the by the end of this fourth turning we
have a very high conviction belief that
the Federal Reserve's principal mandate
will be supporting the Treasury market
functioning more so than you know
maintaining stable prices and more so
than maintaining maximum employment. We
think those will become less uh
important as drivers of Fed policy than
the functioning uh in the Treasury
market. And so if we you know kind of
skating to where the buck's going to be,
none of this really matters. you what
really matters is okay where is Fed
policy going to go from a structural
perspective we talked about that uh uh
at we've been talking about that out in
Aussie for years now in context of our
investing doing a 420 regime uh analysis
which perfectly prepared our clients 42
macro clients for the w the very wide
wider range of of wider distribution
rather of economic and policy outcomes
that we continue to observe uh from a
fiscal and monetary and ultimately asset
price response perspective here in this
forturning regime and obviously in our
view the forturning has got materially
accelerated here in 2025. So I think
when you think about Fed policy you know
we can think about we can talk about
inflation we can talk about growth in
the labor market but ultimately where
we're headed uh if you want to you know
if you want to finish the race before
all the other investors where we're
headed is the Treasury is going to LBO
the Fed and ultimately it's the Fed's
going to be used as a tool to support
Treasury market functioning because
that's how these things always end.
That's paradigm D. That's the print
phase. Right now, we're in paradigm C,
which is the growth phase. And, you
know, courtesy of uh courtesy of some
fantastic research from from yours
truly. Uh there's investors all over the
world benefiting from our paradigm C
theme. And in our view, we think they're
going to be benefiting from our paradigm
C theme on a trend basis, at least
through the midterm elections next year.
So, uh really happy to uh have beat Wall
Street yet again to a a very material
theme. uh really grateful for our
clients for partnering with us uh to
participate in the persistent and
consistent front running of Wall Street
to these important themes uh in global
financial markets and uh don't expect
that's going to end anytime soon uh Lord
willing. So we'll wrap it up there.
